Post the original `INV-12-completed:<sid>` operator-handoff notice (idempotent) and `return 0`. A `none` verdict here means a review SHOULD have run against this PR and something prevented it (crashed review wrapper, actor-predicate mismatch) ‚Äî a human should look, exactly as [INV-12] originally intended.
2. **No PR exists**: mirror `failed-substantive`'s Branch C ([INV-35]) exactly ‚Äî `acquire_dispatch_marker <issue> dev-new` FIRST ([INV-108]), post `INV-12-no-pr-fresh-dev:<sid>` (idempotent, distinct marker text ‚Äî no review failed, so `INV-35-fresh-dev` would misrepresent the cause), truncate the per-issue log via `_reset_session_log` (fail-closed: operator comment + `release_dispatch_marker` + `return 0` on truncate failure), `label_swap pending-dev ‚Üí in-progress`, `post_dispatch_token dev-new`, explicit dispatch-rc handling (`is_dispatch_deferred_rc` ‚Üí `handle_dispatch_deferred`; any other nonzero ‚Üí `release_dispatch_marker` + `return 0`; rc=0 ‚Üí `dispatch_marker_confirm_launched`). **No per-HEAD attempt marker is recorded** (unlike Branch C) ‚Äî there is no HEAD to key one on, so `MAX_RETRIES` alone is the bound.

**Why**: `none` means "no qualifying review comment was found at all" in the actor+timestamp window ‚Äî the classifier's sole `none`-return path. This occurs both when no PR was ever created (no review could run) and when a PR exists but review never posted a qualifying comment. Pre-fix, BOTH cases fell into the same permanent, unbounded silent park ‚Äî the only completed-session verdict branch with zero bound of any kind (`failed-substantive` has the four-stage A/B/B‚Ä≤/B‚Ä≥ ladder plus Branch C's `MAX_RETRIES`; `failed-non-substantive` has `REVIEW_RETRY_LIMIT`; `none` had nothing). A dev session that ends its turn cleanly without ever opening a PR (e.g. the agent decides to "wait for a background test run" and never resumes) is the textbook shape: the wrapper flips the label back to `pending-dev` with a WARNING comment, but the next tick's `none` classification (no PR ‚áí no review ‚áí no qualifying comment) posted a one-time operator notice and did nothing further, forever (live reproduction: issue #456, this repo, 2026-07-10).

**No escalation ladder for the no-PR branch, by design.** Branches A/B/B‚Ä≥ ([INV-85]/[INV-105]) require a stable HEAD to compare against (same-HEAD + attempt marker, or frozen-HEAD round count); Branch B‚Ä≤ ([INV-92]) requires a review-verdict trailer to classify `dev-actionable`. Both preconditions are structurally absent before a PR exists ‚Äî there is no artifact to compare and no verdict to classify. A flat `MAX_RETRIES` counter is therefore not a *weaker* protection here than the ladder gives `failed-substantive`; it is the *only* protection that is meaningful, because none of the ladder's signals can exist without an artifact or a verdict. This is a bound-by-volume answer, not a convergence answer ‚Äî an accepted tradeoff: every attempt is independently visible via its own `INV-12-no-pr-fresh-dev:<sid>` marker + notice comment, so an operator sees N distinct timestamped attempts before `MAX_RETRIES` trips, never a single ambiguous stall.

**The companion counting fix** (`count_no_pr_attempts()`, see below) is required in the same change ‚Äî without it, `MAX_RETRIES` never actually trips for this branch, because `count_agent_failures` only matches the literal string `"Agent Session Report (Dev)"`, and the wrapper's "Agent exited successfully but no PR was created. Moving to pending-dev for retry." is a SEPARATE, independent WARNING comment that string never matches.

- `count_no_pr_attempts(issue_num)` (`lib-dispatch.sh`) echoes the count of "Agent exited successfully but no PR was created" WARNING comments, using the SAME `last_stalled_at` cutoff `count_agent_failures`/`count_dispatcher_crashes` already use ([INV-05] reset-on-unstall parity).
- `count_retries()` sums it in **unconditionally**, alongside `count_agent_failures` ‚Äî NOT behind the `_agent_started_since_stall` gate that only guards `count_dispatcher_crashes`. The WARNING only ever posts on the wrapper's `AGENT_RAN=true` path, so a no-PR attempt always implies the agent genuinely started; gating it would be redundant, and omitting it would reopen the counting gap.
- `mark_stalled()`'s operator-facing comment gained the new count as an additional named term (`"... + N no-PR retry attempts + ..."`) so the posted arithmetic keeps summing to the real `count_retries()` value.

**Reachability**: with `MAX_RETRIES=3`, the FIRST no-PR completion (the original, pre-branch dispatch that produced the WARNING comment) already counts as attempt 1 before this branch ever runs; the branch's own dispatches are attempts 2 and 3; `dispatcher-tick.sh` Step 4's pre-flight `count_retries() >= MAX_RETRIES` gate (which runs BEFORE `handle_pending_dev_pr_exists`/`handle_completed_session_routing` are ever reached) stalls before a 3rd branch dispatch. Observable sequence: **2 branch-dispatched `dev-new` attempts, then `mark_stalled`** ‚Äî not 3.

**Producer**: `lib-dispatch.sh::handle_completed_session_routing` (the `none)` case), `lib-dispatch.sh::count_no_pr_attempts`, `lib-dispatch.sh::count_retries`, `lib-dispatch.sh::mark_stalled`.
**Consumer**: `dispatcher-tick.sh` Step 4b.5.1 (direct entry) and Step 4a.5's [INV-98] same-HEAD delegation (indirect entry, though that delegation only reaches this router when a PR already exists ‚Äî the same-HEAD precondition itself implies a PR, so the no-PR branch is unreachable via that path; see the disjointness note below).
**Status**: **ENFORCED** (closes #456/#461).
**Test**: `tests/unit/test-issue-461-completed-none-bounded-retry.sh` ‚Äî TC-461-NONE-001..012 (no-PR Branch-C-mirror dispatch sequence, PR-exists/transport-failure regression pins, acquire/truncate/label-swap/dispatch-rc error handling mirroring Branch C's own tests), TC-461-SELFHEAL-001 (the [INV-111] self-heal branch's disjoint precondition is unaffected), TC-461-COUNT-001..005 (`count_no_pr_attempts` correctness, no double-counting against the Session Report, cutoff-reset parity, `count_retries` summation, `mark_stalled` comment text). `tests/unit/test-handle-completed-routing-golden-trace.sh` ‚Äî TC-HCGT-001/002 updated to the PR-exists shape (this harness's PR lookup always resolves a PR, so the `none` arm now begins with the PR-existence check before the unchanged operator-handoff path). `tests/unit/test-mark-stalled-golden-trace.sh` ‚Äî TC-MSGT-007 extended to pin `count_no_pr_attempts` as a literal (non-verb) caller-side call.

**Guards preserved**: [INV-12] unchanged (only what happens after a session is confirmed `completed` with `verdict=none` changes); [INV-05] the new count term shares the existing cutoff; [INV-85]/[INV-92]/[INV-105] not applicable (their preconditions are structurally absent pre-PR); [INV-98] unaffected in the success case, and its own [INV-111] self-heal sub-branch remains independent and disjoint (different marker: `self-heal-lost-session:<head>` vs. this invariant's `INV-12-no-pr-fresh-dev:<sid>` ‚Äî the two answer different questions: "PR exists, was reviewed, but we lost the session" vs. "no PR was ever produced"); [INV-108] the new dispatch uses the identical acquire-before-side-effects / errexit-safe-guard / explicit-rc-branch / confirm-after-launch sequence as Branch C; [INV-13] unaffected (still wrapped by the existing `run_agent` timeout); [INV-45] unaffected, complementary (`needs_open_pr_only` handles "pushed a branch but never opened a PR" on the NEXT dispatch, regardless of how it was triggered ‚Äî a `dev-new` from this branch inherits that fast path for free).

**Cross-references**:
- [INV-12](#inv-12-resume-only-against-unfinished-sessions) ‚Äî the carve-out note there now covers TWO branches (INV-35's substantive/non-substantive routing, and this invariant's no-PR bound), not just the original single-branch handoff.
- [INV-35](#inv-35-review-aware-resume-routing-for-completed-sessions) ‚Äî the routing table's `none` row is re-scoped: PR-exists `none` is unchanged; no-PR `none` now has its own bounded route, documented here rather than duplicated there.
- [INV-111](#inv-111-the-dev-wrappers-session-report-is-the-first-durable-write-in-cleanup-gh-resolution-is-re-armed-per-write-against-a-vanished-auth-shim-and-a-same-head-review-fail-with-no-resolvable-session-id-self-heals-via-a-bounded-fresh-dev-new-instead-of-parking-forever) ‚Äî its self-heal branch's `PR exists + same HEAD` precondition is structurally disjoint from this invariant's `no PR` precondition; the two dev-new-dispatching branches never collide.
- [`docs/designs/issue-456-completed-no-verdict-bounded-retry.md`](../designs/issue-456-completed-no-verdict-bounded-retry.md) ‚Äî the full design with the classifier-semantics correction, the escalation-ladder-applicability argument (Q3), and the rejected alternatives.

---

## INV-125: a resolved dev session id whose completion `is_session_completed` cannot confirm (a non-terminal stop reason such as `api_error`, a non-claude dev CLI, or an unreadable log), with no live wrapper, gets the SAME bounded verdict-aware recovery as the [INV-111] self-heal branch ‚Äî and every marker-present same-HEAD fall-through escalates to `mark_stalled`, never the residual park

_Triage (issue #236): [machine-checked: tests/unit/test-issue-466-crashed-session-recovery.sh, tests/unit/test-issue-351-stale-verdict-delegate.sh]_

**Rule** (issue #466): `handle_pending_dev_pr_exists()`'s same-HEAD block extracts the [INV-111] self-heal case-statement body into a shared helper, `_same_head_verdict_aware_recovery(issue_num, pr_ref, current_head, cause)`, called from TWO disjoint preconditions:

1. **`cause=self-heal`** ([INV-111], unchanged): `extract_dev_session_id` returns empty ‚Äî the session-report comment was lost entirely.
2. **`cause=crashed-session`** (NEW): a session id DID resolve, but `is_session_completed` returned false. This covers a non-terminal stop reason (`api_error`, a Bedrock 5xx mid-response, `stop_sequence`, etc.), any non-claude dev CLI (`is_session_completed` is claude-only by design ‚Äî [INV-101]/[INV-37]), or a missing/unreadable session log.

Both call sites gate on `may_stall_now` (no live dev wrapper) BEFORE invoking the helper ‚Äî a live wrapper must never be raced into a duplicate `dev-new`.

**Semantics ‚Äî this is NOT a "crashed session" determination.** `is_session_completed=false` means completion is **unprovable**, not that the session crashed ‚Äî for a non-terminal stop reason or an unparsed CLI, the prior session may have finished normally. The recovery predicate is narrower and CLI-agnostic: *same reviewed HEAD + no live wrapper + no new commits = no progress, and one bounded, verdict-appropriate retry is safe regardless of WHY completion is unprovable.* This framing is scoped to the same-HEAD/no-progress context and MUST NOT be generalized to call sites that lack it (e.g. first-dispatch decisions), where "not completed" absolutely must not be read as "crashed".

**Verdict-aware routing** (via `classify_recent_review_verdict`, the SAME classifier [INV-35]'s `handle_completed_session_routing` uses, called with an empty `session_end_iso`):
- `dev-actionable=false` ([INV-92]) ‚Üí `mark_stalled`, no `dev-new`. Own per-cause marker (`${cause}-non-actionable:<head>`) ‚Äî never dispatches, so there is no budget to double-spend across causes.
- `passed` (race ‚Äî a `passed` verdict landed but the issue is still `pending-dev`) ‚Üí no-op, mirroring `handle_completed_session_routing`'s own `passed` branch; Step 0 hygiene reconciles.
- `failed-non-substantive` ‚Üí `label_swap pending-dev ‚Üí pending-review` (re-review, not a `dev-new` ‚Äî the code isn't the problem), bounded via the **shared** `self-heal-non-substantive:<head>` marker ‚Äî the SAME marker namespace regardless of which cause requested the re-review, because a re-review costs the same either way; a separate per-cause marker would silently double the budget.
- `failed-substantive` (dev-actionable=true) or `none` (fail OPEN, the same posture the pre-existing self-heal behavior and the classifier's own legacy no-trailer fallback take) ‚Üí bounded `dev-new`, gated on a **shared** budget check across BOTH `self-heal-lost-session:<head>` AND `crashed-session-retry:<head>` ‚Äî either marker present means this HEAD already consumed its one self-heal/crash-recovery `dev-new` with no progress, regardless of which cause is asking now.

**Part 2 ‚Äî closing the counting hole.** Every marker-present / budget-exhausted arm above calls `mark_stalled` **directly** ‚Äî it NEVER falls through to the residual `stale-verdict:<head>` park. In a park, `count_retries` is frozen by construction (a park posts only an idempotent notice, which `count_retries` never counts ‚Äî no dispatch, no countable comment), so the pre-existing code comments claiming "MAX_RETRIES remains the eventual backstop" for a marker-present fall-through were **false as written**: with no dispatch ever running again for that HEAD, no comment `count_retries` scans for can ever be posted, so the counter can never grow and `MAX_RETRIES` can never trip. The marker itself IS the evidence that the one bounded recovery for this HEAD was already spent with no progress ‚Äî that is sufficient grounds for `mark_stalled` on its own, without needing `count_retries` to independently confirm it. (Teaching `count_retries` to count park notices instead was rejected: notices are idempotent, at most one per HEAD, so they could never accumulate to any threshold either ‚Äî the explicit `mark_stalled` call is the only shape that actually closes the hole.)

After this fix, the residual `stale-verdict:<head>` park is reachable ONLY for genuinely transient states: a dev wrapper is still alive (`may_stall_now` defers ‚Äî Step 5 owns liveness), a concurrent tick holds the `dev-new` dispatch marker ([INV-108] ‚Äî the helper returns 1, distinct from its `mark_stalled` arms which return 0), or the helper's own comment-read preflight fails (below ‚Äî the helper also returns 1 here, same as the [INV-108] case).

**Comment-fetch-failure preflight (PR #471 review fix).** Every downstream check in the helper ‚Äî `classify_recent_review_verdict`, and the `self-heal-lost-session`/`crashed-session-retry`/`self-heal-non-substantive` marker-present probes ‚Äî reads `itp_list_comments` and treats an EMPTY result as a legitimate negative (`verdict=none`, "marker absent"). Left unguarded, a transient comment-fetch failure (rate-limit/auth/network blip) produces the exact same empty shape, so it would misclassify into `mark_stalled` (a fetch failure looks like "budget already spent" ‚Äî the marker-present checks default fail-CLOSED via `${_budget_spent:-1}`/`${_ns_present:-1}`, i.e. *empty read ‚Üí treated as marker PRESENT*) or an unwarranted fresh `dev-new` (a fetch failure looks like `verdict=none`, which fails OPEN to a dispatch) against an otherwise-healthy issue ‚Äî precisely the transient class this invariant reserved the residual park for. Fix: `_same_head_verdict_aware_recovery` preflights `itp_list_comments` once at entry; a non-zero rc returns 1 immediately (before touching `classify_recent_review_verdict` or any marker probe), so the caller falls through to the unchanged residual park instead of misrouting. A same-HEAD branch is only reached after a review-FAILED verdict has already posted at least one comment, so a genuinely empty comment list can never be legitimate here ‚Äî any empty/failed read at this call site is definitionally transient.

**Why** (#466): before this fix, `is_session_completed=false` with a *resolved* session id had no recovery arm at all ‚Äî the `if [ -z "$_sid" ]` precondition of the pre-existing [INV-111] self-heal branch never matched (a resolved-but-unprovable session id is not the "lost session-report comment" shape self-heal exists for), so execution fell straight to the unconditional residual park. Because the park only unblocks on a new commit, and the HEAD is already-reviewed (so no dev-new will ever be dispatched to produce one), this was a permanent silent deadlock ‚Äî the `api_error` sibling of the `verdict=none` permanent-park bug [INV-123] fixed: same "only branch with no bound of any kind" shape, different entry condition. Live reproduction: a dev session terminated by a transient provider-side API error mid-response, with no new commit, left its issue parked in `pending-dev` for ~23h before an operator noticed (downstream consumer project, 2026-07-10).

**Producer**: `lib-dispatch.sh::_same_head_verdict_aware_recovery` (the shared helper), `lib-dispatch.sh::handle_pending_dev_pr_exists` (both call sites).
**Consumer**: `dispatcher-tick.sh` Step 4a.5 (via `handle_pending_dev_pr_exists`'s same-HEAD block).
**Status**: **ENFORCED** (closes #466).
**Test**: `tests/unit/test-issue-466-crashed-session-recovery.sh` ‚Äî TC-466-CRASH-001/002 (the `api_error` / non-claude-CLI regression ‚Äî both reach the identical helper code path since `is_session_completed` is stubbed at the return-code level, so a separate unreadable-log pin adds no behavioral coverage; dispatches a bounded `dev-new` instead of parking ‚Äî fails before the fix), TC-466-VERDICT-001..003 (verdict-aware routing pins ‚Äî the `failed-substantive`/`none` dev-new arm is exercised directly by CRASH-001/002, not a separate pin), TC-466-BUDGET-001..003 (shared-budget pins across both causes and both marker types), TC-466-STALL-001..003 (the counting-hole regression ‚Äî marker-present reaches `mark_stalled` independent of `count_retries`), TC-466-LIVE-001 (a live wrapper still yields the unchanged residual park, with its updated non-permanent-park comment text), TC-466-INV108-001..004 (incl. 003b) ([INV-108] dispatch-marker concurrency/failure-rc sequencing, mirroring the adjacent [INV-111] self-heal branch's own tests), TC-466-FETCH-001/002 (PR #471 review follow-up: a transient `itp_list_comments` failure, for both the `crashed-session` and `self-heal` causes, hits the residual park ‚Äî never `mark_stalled`, never a `dev-new`). `tests/unit/test-issue-351-stale-verdict-delegate.sh` ‚Äî TC-351-DELEG-6/6-LIVE and TC-351-DELEG-7b/7b-RECOVER updated: the non-claude-CLI and not-completed cases now assert the new `crashed-session-retry:<head>` dev-new dispatch (not the old permanent park) when no wrapper is alive, with dedicated pins preserving the live-wrapper-defers-to-park behavior; TC-351-DELEG-7a-SELFHEAL-BOUND and TC-351-DELEG-7a-SELFHEAL-NONSUB-BOUND updated to assert `mark_stalled` instead of the park (Part 2).

**Guards preserved**: [INV-98]/[INV-111]/[INV-123] untouched ‚Äî the new `crashed-session` cause slots into the same helper via a disjoint precondition (`_sid` non-empty vs. empty), never colliding with either. [INV-108]: the new dispatch call site uses the identical acquire-before-side-effects / errexit-safe-guard / explicit-rc-branch / confirm-after-launch sequence as the adjacent [INV-111] self-heal dispatch. [INV-26]: wrapper liveness via the shared `may_stall_now` predicate ‚Äî no new liveness logic. [INV-92]: `dev-actionable=false` still escalates, never burns a `dev-new`, for either cause. [INV-91]: all issue/PR I/O routes through existing `itp_*`/`chp_*` verbs; zero raw `gh`. [INV-105]: not applicable ‚Äî that breaker's precondition (`handle_completed_session_routing`'s `failed-substantive` ladder) requires a `completed` session, structurally disjoint from this invariant's `not completed` precondition.

**Cross-references**:
- [INV-111](#inv-111-the-dev-wrappers-session-report-is-the-first-durable-write-in-cleanup-gh-resolution-is-re-armed-per-write-against-a-vanished-auth-shim-and-a-same-head-review-fail-with-no-resolvable-session-id-self-heals-via-a-bounded-fresh-dev-new-instead-of-parking-forever) ‚Äî the self-heal branch this invariant's `_same_head_verdict_aware_recovery` helper extracts and shares; its `cause=self-heal` call site is now the helper's OTHER disjoint precondition, unchanged in behavior except that its own marker-present fall-through now also escalates to `mark_stalled` (Part 2, this invariant) instead of the residual park.
- [INV-123](#inv-123-the-completed-session-verdict-none-route-is-bounded--a-no-pr-completed-session-retries-via-dev-new-under-max_retries-branch-c-mirror-no-escalation-ladder-and-a-pr-exists-none-no-qualifying-review-comment-found-still-fails-closed-to-the-inv-12-operator-handoff) ‚Äî the sibling permanent-park bug this invariant fixes for a different entry condition (a resolved-but-unprovable session id vs. a `verdict=none` completed session); the two share the same "only branch with no bound" shape.
- [INV-92](#inv-92-a-review-blocking-finding-the-dev-agent-provably-cannot-act-on-protected-path--missing-token-scope-is-not-routed-to-dev-resume--the-wrapper-classifies-each-findings-actionability-and-the-dispatcher-escalates-a-non-actionable-verdict-to-stalled) ‚Äî the `dev-actionable=false` escalation this invariant's helper reuses (own per-cause marker, no dev-new).
- [INV-108](#inv-108-every-dispatcher-tick-dispatch-site-acquires-a-controller-side-per-issuemode-marker-atomically-before-any-side-effect--a-losing-acquire-skips-cleanly-never-dispatches-the-marker-expires-via-ttl-never-wedging-the-issue-the-dispatch-token-gains-a-run-field-for-post-hoc-attribution) ‚Äî the controller-side dedup guard the shared helper's dev-new dispatch adopts, identical sequencing to the pre-existing self-heal call site.
- [`docs/designs/issue-466-crashed-session-recovery.md`](../designs/issue-466-crashed-session-recovery.md) ‚Äî the full design, including the rejected "unconditional dev-new" and "separate per-cause markers" alternatives.

---

## INV-124: the PR-diff-size (over-reach) signal is a default-off, fail-open soft signal never read by verdict aggregation

_Triage (issue #236): [machine-checked: tests/unit/test-lib-review-diffcap.sh, tests/unit/test-chp-pr-diffstat.sh]_

**Rule**: `PR_DIFF_SOFT_CAP_FILES` / `PR_DIFF_SOFT_CAP_LINES` (`autonomous.conf`, both default unset) each independently gate one dimension of a cheap, deterministic pre-review diff-size check. When BOTH are unset, the feature is fully disabled: `build_review_prompt()`'s output is byte-identical to pre-#452, no `chp_pr_diffstat` provider-seam call is made, and no `pr_diff_soft_cap` metrics event fires. Each key normalizes independently (`lib-review-diffcap.sh::_diff_cap_normalize`) ‚Äî empty/unset, `0`, negative, and non-numeric all normalize to empty (disabled for that key), silently, never a startup error.

When at least one cap is set, the wrapper computes, ONCE per review round BEFORE the fan-out (`autonomous-review.sh`, NOT once per fan-out member ‚Äî `build_review_prompt()` is called once per member, so a per-member computation would redundantly re-read the provider seam and duplicate the metrics row):

```
over_reach = (FILES cap set AND changed_files > FILES cap)
          OR (LINES cap set AND changed_lines > LINES cap)
```

Strict `>` (never `>=` ‚Äî a stat exactly at the cap does NOT trigger). The comparison is pure (`lib-review-diffcap.sh::review_diff_over_reach`); the diff-stat read is a NEW provider-seam verb, `chp_pr_diffstat PR DIMENSIONS-CSV` (¬ß3.2, `docs/pipeline/provider-spec.md`) ‚Äî GitHub answers both dimensions from one `gh pr view --json additions,deletions,changedFiles` call regardless of which is requested (zero marginal cost); GitLab answers `files` from the already-fetched base MR view's `changes_count` (parsing the capped `"1000+"` string down to the integer `1000`) and `lines` from a SEPARATE GraphQL `diffStatsSummary` call issued ONLY when `lines` is actually requested (pay-only-if-configured; GitLab's GraphQL endpoint is a distinct transport primitive, `_gl_graphql`, since it authenticates via `Authorization: Bearer` rather than the REST `PRIVATE-TOKEN` header the rest of the GitLab leaves use).

**Fail-open, never fabricates a warning.** A diff-stat read failure (provider-seam rc‚âÝ0, empty, or unparseable) leaves that dimension's stat unset; `review_diff_over_reach` treats an unset stat as unreadable and never contributes `true` for it, regardless of whether its cap is configured. On GitLab specifically, a GraphQL failure (auth/network/schema error) degrades ONLY the `lines` dimension to unreadable ‚Äî it does NOT suppress a `files` result the base-MR-view read already obtained successfully (independent failure domains; `chp_gitlab_pr_diffstat` returns `{changed_files: N}` at rc 0 even when the GraphQL call for `lines` failed).

**Soft signal, never a gate.** `over_reach` is a prompt-weighting advisory ONLY: a pure `echo`-only helper (`lib-review-diffcap.sh::review_diff_soft_cap_prompt_note`, mirroring `lib-review-classify.sh::review_protected_paths_prompt_rule`) renders a markdown note ‚Äî naming the measured stat(s), the exceeded dimension(s), and stating explicitly that this is advisory ‚Äî interpolated into `build_review_prompt()`'s heredoc; empty output when `over_reach=false`. `over_reach` is NEVER read by any verdict-aggregation code path, any `_classify_*_gate` function, or the PASS/FAIL/merge decision ‚Äî a legitimately large PR (migration, refactor) reaches PASS and auto-merges exactly as if this feature did not exist, regardless of `over_reach`'s value. Exactly one `pr_diff_soft_cap` metrics event (`lib-metrics.sh`, `docs/pipeline/metrics.md`) is emitted per enabled review round ‚Äî NOT folded into the per-fan-out-member `review_agent_run` event (which fires once per agent; folding a PR-level flag into it would duplicate the same value N times and corrupt `metrics-report.sh`'s aggregation).

**Why**: Anthropic's Loop Engineering guidance names two failure modes to watch when running loops ‚Äî **stall** (agent stuck) and **over-reach** (agent doing too much). This pipeline's stall detection is thorough (grace window, near-success cross-checks, PID heartbeat); over-reach had no signal at all ‚Äî as long as CI is green and a PR exists, the mechanical gates approve it, so a dev agent could silently expand a small issue into a sweeping multi-file change. The review side already has requirement-drift detection (issue-comment scope changes), but that catches *changed* requirements, not an *oversized* diff against unchanged requirements. Diff size is only a heuristic PROXY for "did this PR grow beyond what the issue described" ‚Äî large diffs are sometimes correct (migrations, refactors, this repo's own Lane-GC / provider-seam series), so the signal must weight reviewer attention, never veto.

**Amendment note (2026-07-11, review round 2)**: round-1 review caught that `_gl_graphql` (the `lines`-dimension transport primitive) hard-required `GITLAB_TOKEN` with no path through `GITLAB_TRANSPORT_HOOK` at all ‚Äî a hook-only, token-less GitLab installation could authenticate every OTHER leaf via its own transport but could never compute `changed_lines`, contradicting this invariant's "both dimensions supported on both hosts" framing (cost differs, capability does not). Fixed by giving `_gl_graphql` an OPTIONAL second hook point, `_gl_graphql_hook` (`providers/lib-gitlab-transport.sh`) ‚Äî see [INV-116](#inv-116-the-gitlab-transport-is-a-two-layer-choke-point-_gl_http-request-primitive--_gl_api-public-function-pagination--backoff--fail-closed-live-in-_gl_api-the-override-hook-may-redefine-_gl_http-only)'s own amendment note for the transport-layer mechanics. This does not change any rule stated above ‚Äî `over_reach`, the fail-open behavior, the metrics event, and the soft-signal-never-a-gate posture are all unaffected; only the GitLab `lines`-dimension READ PATH gained an additional, opt-in auth route.

**Producer**: `lib-review-diffcap.sh` (`_diff_cap_normalize`, `review_diff_soft_cap_dimensions_needed`, `review_diff_over_reach`, `review_diff_soft_cap_prompt_note` ‚Äî pure, no I/O), `autonomous-review.sh` (the once-per-round computation + `chp_pr_diffstat` call + `metrics_emit pr_diff_soft_cap` + the prompt interpolation), `lib-code-host.sh`/`providers/chp-github.sh`/`providers/chp-gitlab.sh` (`chp_pr_diffstat` shim + leaves), `providers/lib-gitlab-transport.sh` (`_gl_graphql` + its optional `_gl_graphql_hook` probe).
**Consumer**: the review agent (reads the advisory note, may weight its findings accordingly ‚Äî never required to act on it); `metrics-report.sh` (informational aggregation only, per `docs/pipeline/metrics.md`).
**Status**: **ENFORCED**.
**Test**: `tests/unit/test-lib-review-diffcap.sh` (TC-OVERREACH-001..016 ‚Äî normalize/dimensions-needed/over-reach OR-logic/boundary/prompt-note/byte-identical-when-disabled), `tests/unit/test-chp-pr-diffstat.sh` (TC-OVERREACH-007..012 ‚Äî GitHub single-call contract, GitLab `changes_count`/`"1000+"` parsing, GitLab conditional-GraphQL-call gating, GitLab independent-failure-domain degrade), `tests/unit/test-provider-cutover.sh` (no new raw `gh pr view` in `autonomous-review.sh`), `tests/unit/test-lib-gitlab-transport.sh` (TC-GLT-093..101 ‚Äî the `_gl_graphql_hook` optional override amendment, including the round-2/round-3 fail-closed shape validation on the hook path).

**Guards preserved**: [INV-70] (metrics emission stays observe-only ‚Äî a `pr_diff_soft_cap` emit failure can never change the wrapper's exit code, label transition, or verdict); [INV-91] (the diff-stat read goes through the provider seam, `check-provider-cutover.sh` stays green); [INV-116] (the GitLab transport's `_gl_http`/`_gl_api` two-layer preflight/fail-loud contract is unchanged; `_gl_graphql` is a sibling primitive alongside it, not a modification of it; the round-2 `_gl_graphql_hook` addition is a strictly additive, optional override on `_gl_graphql` only).

---

## INV-126: fail-closed when no wall-clock timeout binary is available

_Triage (issue #236): [machine-checked: tests/unit/test-agent-timeout-wrapper.sh]_

**Rule**: `lib-agent.sh` MUST refuse to launch an agent ‚Äî aborting at SOURCE TIME with `ADT_CFG_TIMEOUT_TOOL_MISSING` ‚Äî when neither coreutils `timeout` nor macOS `gtimeout` is found on `PATH`, unless the operator has explicitly opted into the watchdog fallback (`AGENT_TIMEOUT_WATCHDOG_FALLBACK=true`) **AND** `setsid` is also available on `PATH`. If the watchdog fallback is opted in but `setsid` is missing too, the watchdog cannot establish a killable process group, so this combination fails closed identically to the plain default ‚Äî it is never a WARN-and-proceed. This replaces the pre-#451 behavior of a one-time WARN followed by an unbounded (no wall-clock cap) agent invocation.

**Placement, decided**: the authoritative detection and fail-closed abort live in `lib-agent.sh`'s top-level (source-time) code, mirroring [INV-38](#inv-38-per-side-agent_launcher-precedence)'s `AGENT_DEV_LAUNCHER`/`AGENT_REVIEW_LAUNCHER` CLI-mismatch guard exactly ‚Äî same `error_surface "$(error_peek_issue_arg "$@")"` call shape, same `return 1 2>/dev/null || exit 1` abort. This is deliberate and load-bearing: `lib-agent.sh` is sourced by `autonomous-dev.sh`/`autonomous-review.sh`, and those wrapper processes run on the **execution host** under every `EXECUTION_BACKEND` ‚Äî `local` (dispatcher and wrapper are the same host) or `remote-aws-ssm` (`dispatch-remote-aws-ssm.sh` is "purely a transport layer"; the wrapper process it triggers via SSM `send-command` runs on the remote box). A check placed in `dispatcher-tick.sh` instead would test the wrong machine under `remote-aws-ssm` ‚Äî the same "PID file lives on Box B but the dispatcher runs on Box A" bug class `dispatcher-flow.md` already documents from #137. Detection is resolved **once**, shared between the dev (`AGENT_TIMEOUT`, 4h default) and review (`AGENT_REVIEW_TIMEOUT`, 1h default) call sites ‚Äî binary presence is orthogonal to which duration value is in effect, so there is exactly one `_AGENT_TIMEOUT_CMD` resolution, not two.

**Why**: [INV-13](#inv-13-wall-clock-cap-on-agent-invocations)'s `_run_with_timeout` is the **only** hard per-run bound anywhere in this system ‚Äî there is no `--max-turns` and no token gate. Pre-#451, a missing `timeout`/`gtimeout` degraded to a WARN-and-proceed: the wrapper ran the agent CLI with **zero** wall-clock protection, silently removing the last containment for a runaway agent (SSE keepalive without a stop event, an MCP stdio deadlock, a DNS/TCP black hole ‚Äî the same failure modes INV-13 itself cites). This is most likely to bite a fresh macOS host, where `timeout` does not exist at all and `gtimeout` requires `brew install coreutils`.

**Watchdog fallback (opt-in, default OFF)**: `AGENT_TIMEOUT_WATCHDOG_FALLBACK=true` accepts a pure-shell `sleep $AGENT_TIMEOUT && kill` in place of refusing to launch ‚Äî but ONLY when `setsid` is also present at source time (see below). Implemented inside `_run_with_timeout` itself (not a separate call site): when `_AGENT_TIMEOUT_CMD` is empty and the toggle is on, a `setsid`-isolated background job sleeps for `AGENT_TIMEOUT` (converted to seconds via `_timeout_value_to_seconds`, since a non-GNU `sleep` may reject `h`/`m`/`d` suffixes) and then kills the **process group** ‚Äî `_AGENT_RUN_PID` (the `setsid` session leader = PGID, exactly as `_run_with_timeout` already establishes for the coreutils-`timeout` path), never a lone PID. It escalates TERM ‚Üí bounded 30s grace ‚Üí KILL via a self-contained TERM‚Üígrace‚ÜíKILL body (the same shape `_kill_group_escalate`, `lib-lane.sh`, [INV-114](#inv-114-every-pipeline-initiated-kill-escalates-term--bounded-grace--sigkill-gated-on-groupscope-emptiness-never-on-leader-liveness-alone) defines), writing a result marker ("124" before the TERM, "137" if the grace window elapses and KILL is needed) so `_run_with_timeout` can observe whether the watchdog actually fired. The watchdog job is deliberately NOT disowned (see round-3 below) and is cancelled (with no lingering marker) once the wrapped command finishes on its own before the watchdog ever wakes ‚Äî so a fast-finishing command never leaves a stale deferred kill armed against a PGID number that could be reused by a later spawn.

**Watchdog reconciliation is a wait-for-completion, not a fire-and-forget cancel (PR #469 review round-2, both [P1])**: once `_run_with_timeout`'s own `wait` on the wrapped command returns, it checks the result marker rather than unconditionally killing the watchdog job:
- **Marker present (watchdog already fired)**: `_run_with_timeout` does NOT cancel the watchdog ‚Äî it `wait`s for the watchdog job itself to finish its own escalation (TERM ‚Üí grace ‚Üí KILL) before returning. Cancelling immediately on the wrapped command's own `wait` returning was the original bug: a TERM-obeying session **leader** can die and unblock that `wait` while a TERM-**ignoring descendant** is still alive, mid-grace, with the watchdog's own pending KILL step not yet reached ‚Äî killing the watchdog job at that moment abandoned the KILL follow-through, leaving the descendant to survive indefinitely past the function's return. There is no marker-write race here: the marker is written **before** the corresponding signal is sent (not after), so the only way the wrapped command's `wait` can unblock as a *result* of the watchdog's TERM is if the marker was already on disk first.
- **Marker absent (watchdog never fired)**: safe to cancel immediately ‚Äî this is the original fast-finish case (a command that completes well within `AGENT_TIMEOUT`), unchanged from the initial #451 implementation.
- **Exit-code normalization**: on a marker hit, `_run_with_timeout` overwrites its own captured exit code with the marker's value (124 or 137) instead of the raw signal-death status `wait` reports for the wrapped command (e.g. 143 for a plain `SIGTERM` death that the process didn't trap). This matters because downstream consumers key off the exact 124/137 contract coreutils `timeout` establishes ‚Äî `_classify_noverdict_agent` (`lib-review-aggregate.sh`, [INV-48](#inv-48-per-side-review-wall-clock-timeout-agentreviewtimeout-1h-default-with-browser-e2e-exclusion-and-timeout-veto)) only recognizes 124/137 as `timed-out` (a deciding-FAIL veto); an un-normalized 143 would have fallen through to `unavailable` (silently dropped from the review vote) instead of vetoing the merge. `count_agent_failures`'s pre-existing 124/137 exclusions (dispatcher retry-budget accounting) get the same correct classification for free.

**The watchdog job must NOT be `disown`ed (PR #469 review round-3 [P1])**: the round-2 fix above depends on `wait "$_watchdog_pid"` genuinely BLOCKING until the watchdog's own escalation finishes. The first implementation of round-2 disowned the watchdog job (reasoning, at the time, that the leader's own `wait "$_AGENT_RUN_PID"` must never block on it) ‚Äî but bash's `wait <pid>` on an already-disowned job returns immediately with rc 0 without waiting for it (the disowned PID is dropped from the shell's job table; an untracked job can't be waited on). That silently re-opened BOTH round-2 defects: the reconciliation `wait` returned instantly instead of blocking through the grace-then-KILL escalation, so a TERM-obeying leader with a still-alive TERM-ignoring descendant left the descendant unreaped AND the returned rc stayed at the leader's own 124/143 instead of being normalized to 137. The fix is simply to not `disown` the watchdog job at all ‚Äî an un-disowned sibling background job does not make a *targeted* `wait <specific-pid>` on the leader block on it (verified against bash 5.3: `wait` given an explicit PID waits only for that PID, regardless of what else is in the job table), so the original "never blocks on it" property holds without disown. TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-027 is the regression test for exactly this scenario (TERM-obeying leader, TERM-ignoring descendant) ‚Äî it fails deterministically with a re-introduced `disown` and passes with the fix.

**A rescinded marker must not be replayed as a stale timeout (PR #469 review round-4 [P1])**: the watchdog writes its 124 marker BEFORE calling `kill -TERM`, so `_run_with_timeout`'s reconciliation block can observe a non-empty marker even when the wrapped command finished naturally at the exact instant the watchdog woke ‚Äî in that race, the watchdog's own `kill -TERM` finds the group already gone, and its body rescinds by deleting the marker file (`rm -f "$3" "$3.tmp"`) before exiting 0. The round-2/3 reconciliation code read the marker once before `wait "$_watchdog_pid"` (to decide whether to block) and, after the blocking `wait` returned, re-read it with `cat "$_wd_result_file" 2>/dev/null || echo "$_wd_marker"` ‚Äî the `||` fallback meant a `cat` failure (the expected outcome of a successful rescind, since the file no longer exists) silently substituted the STALE pre-wait value back in, so a genuine natural finish was relabelled as a fabricated 124 timeout. This could wrongly veto a review run or fail a dev run that in fact completed normally. The fix: after `wait "$_watchdog_pid"`, only overwrite `_rc` when the marker file still exists (`[[ -s "$_wd_result_file" ]]`) ‚Äî a missing file after the wait is treated as authoritative proof of a rescind, and `_rc` keeps the natural exit code already captured by `wait "$_AGENT_RUN_PID"` before the watchdog block ran at all.

**A boundary-tie must not read as "watchdog never fired" (PR #469 review round-5 [P1])**: the watchdog does not write its marker until AFTER its deadline `sleep "$1"` returns ‚Äî there is a real scheduling gap between the watchdog waking up and the marker landing on disk. The round-2..4 reconciliation decided whether to block-for-escalation or cancel-immediately based SOLELY on marker presence: an empty marker was treated as proof the watchdog "never fired", safe to cancel outright. That inference is unsound exactly at the deadline boundary ‚Äî if the wrapped command's session LEADER exits naturally at essentially the same instant `AGENT_TIMEOUT` elapses, `wait "$_AGENT_RUN_PID"` can unblock and reach the marker check before the watchdog (already past its sleep, about to signal) has written anything. Reconciliation then wrongly concluded "nothing to protect" and killed the watchdog job outright, abandoning its pending TERM/KILL escalation and leaking a still-alive TERM-ignoring descendant past this function's return with an un-normalized natural rc ‚Äî the same abandonment class round-2/round-3 already fixed for the marker-present case, reopened at the marker-*absent* boundary. The fix: the cancel decision now ALSO checks the process GROUP's own liveness (`kill -0 -- "-$_AGENT_RUN_PID"`) ‚Äî a still-alive group defers to the watchdog exactly like a fired marker would, even with no marker on disk yet, since the watchdog is either about to write one or already mid-write. This makes the block-vs-cancel DECISION robust to a `mktemp` failure (`_wd_result_file=""`) ‚Äî group liveness alone decides whether a surviving descendant needs the watchdog's escalation ‚Äî but NOT the subsequent rc normalization once blocked (see round-6 below, which closes that separate gap). TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-029 (using the test-only `_AGENT_WATCHDOG_WAKE_DELAY_SECS` seam to widen the wake-to-marker window) is the regression test ‚Äî it fails deterministically (rc stays at the leader's natural 0, descendant survives) without the group-liveness check and passes with it.

**`mktemp` failure must not silently defeat rc normalization (PR #469 review round-6 [P1])**: round-5 made the block-vs-cancel *decision* robust to `mktemp` failing (group liveness stands in for marker presence), but once reconciliation decides to block, `_rc` was normalized EXCLUSIVELY by re-reading `$_wd_result_file` ‚Äî if `mktemp` failed at spawn time, `_wd_result_file` is empty for the entire life of the call, so there is no marker to read, ever. The watchdog still runs and still kills the group correctly (kill signals target the PGID directly ‚Äî no file needed for that half), but `_rc` fell all the way back to the wrapped command's raw natural/signal-death status (e.g. `0` for a TERM-obeying leader, `143` for a plain untrapped TERM) instead of `124`/`137`, silently defeating the same INV-48 review-veto / `count_agent_failures` 124/137 contract round-2 established. The fix: the watchdog's OWN exit status is a second, filesystem-independent result channel that mirrors the marker contract exactly ‚Äî the watchdog body now `exit`s `0` (rescinded), `124` (TERM reaped it), or `137` (KILL reaped it), matching the values it would otherwise have written to the marker at each corresponding point. Reconciliation captures `wait "$_watchdog_pid"`'s own rc (`_wd_wait_rc`) on BOTH the block-for-escalation path AND the cancel path (unified ‚Äî previously only the block path captured it at all); when `_wd_result_file` is empty, `_rc` is normalized from `_wd_wait_rc` instead (only when it is exactly `124` or `137` ‚Äî a `0` or a plain signal-death like `143` means "keep the natural `_rc`" already captured, mirroring round-4's rescind semantics). When the marker file mechanism IS available, it remains the sole source of truth per rounds 2-5, unchanged.

A first attempt at this fix (captured `_wd_wait_rc` on the block path only) missed a second, narrower race that a follow-up review pass caught empirically: with `mktemp` failing AND a plain TERM-obeying leader with NO surviving descendant (the direct `mktemp`-failure analog of TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-025's shape), the watchdog's own `kill -TERM` reaps the ENTIRE group before reconciliation's `kill -0` liveness check runs ‚Äî so control fell into the CANCEL branch (group already looks dead), which, in the first attempt, discarded the watchdog's exit code unconditionally instead of consulting it. Worse, the cancel branch's own `kill "$_watchdog_pid"` could race the watchdog's in-flight commitment: if it landed between the watchdog's own `kill -TERM` and its `exit 124` a few lines later, the external TERM killed the watchdog via a raw signal-death (rc `143`) before it ever reached that statement, discarding the correct `124`. Two changes close this: (1) the watchdog body now installs `trap "" TERM` on itself right after its deadline sleep(s) return ‚Äî `sleep "$1"` (the real `AGENT_TIMEOUT` deadline) plus `sleep "$6"` (the test-only wake-delay seam, zero in production) ‚Äî i.e. once committed to evaluating/firing; a cancel signal arriving before that point still kills it promptly (preserving the TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-022 fast-finish behavior), but a cancel arriving after is a harmless no-op, so the watchdog always reaches its own `exit 0`/`124`/`137`, never a raw signal-death; (2) reconciliation always captures `wait`'s rc, on the block path AND the cancel path alike (a `kill` sent to an already-exited job is itself a harmless no-op; `wait` on it still returns the job's real exit code), so the `124`/`137` fallback normalization applies uniformly regardless of which branch reconciliation took.

TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-032 is the regression test: a plain TERM-obeying leader with no descendant (mirroring TC-025 exactly, including the real 30s grace default ‚Äî a shrunk grace combined with a TERM-obeying leader is itself a `kill -0`-right-after-`kill -TERM` timing race independent of this fix, which is why TC-026/027's shrunk-grace pattern is deliberately NOT reused here), run under a stubbed `mktemp` that always fails. It fails deterministically (rc stays at the leader's natural `0`) without either half of the fix and passes with both.

**`setsid`-missing degrades the opt-in to fail-closed, not a WARN-and-proceed**: the watchdog's kill targets the `setsid`-established process GROUP. Without `setsid` on `PATH`, `_AGENT_RUN_PID` is an ordinary PID, not a session leader/PGID, so the watchdog's group-form kill would silently find nothing to signal ‚Äî the agent would run genuinely unbounded despite the operator having opted into a bound. This platform combination (coreutils AND util-linux both missing) is realistic, not hypothetical, on a bare macOS host. The source-time guard therefore treats `AGENT_TIMEOUT_WATCHDOG_FALLBACK=true` + no `setsid` identically to the plain default (no `_AGENT_TIMEOUT_CMD`, watchdog not opted in): `error_surface ... ADT_CFG_TIMEOUT_TOOL_MISSING` and `return 1 2>/dev/null || exit 1`. A PR review pass on #451 flagged the original implementation's WARN-and-proceed in this branch as a P1 blocking finding ‚Äî it left the run unbounded while the log implied a mechanism was active.

**Observability**: the active mechanism (`timeout`, `gtimeout`, `watchdog-fallback`, or `fail-closed-abort`) is logged once at `lib-agent.sh` source time, in the same `[lib-agent]` log-line style the prior WARN used.

**Producer**: `lib-agent.sh` top-level init block (detection + fail-closed abort) and `lib-agent.sh::_run_with_timeout` (watchdog spawn/cancel).
**Consumer**: `run_agent` / `resume_agent` (both call sites are downstream of the source-time abort, so neither needs its own guard) and every `_run_with_timeout` caller (dev run, review fan-out members, browser-E2E lane, smoke probes) uniformly.
**Status**: **ENFORCED** (closes #451).
**Test**: `tests/unit/test-agent-timeout-wrapper.sh` ‚Äî TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-001..005 (detection + shared-resolution + mechanism logging), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-004b (watchdog opted in but `setsid` also missing ‚Üí fail-closed abort, not a WARN), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-010..013 (fail-closed `error_surface` wiring, issue-arg peek, dispatcher-alert fallback), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-020..022 (watchdog PGID kill, descendant reaping, cancel-on-fast-finish), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-025 (TERM-expiry rc normalized to 124, not a raw signal-death status), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-026 (KILL-escalation rc normalized to 137 AND `_run_with_timeout` blocks until the group is actually reaped, not abandoned mid-grace, in the TERM-ignoring-LEADER shape), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-027 (the TERM-obeying-LEADER / TERM-ignoring-DESCENDANT shape ‚Äî the specific regression the round-3 disown fix targets), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-028 (rescinded-marker race ‚Äî a natural finish that races the watchdog's own `kill -TERM` must keep the natural rc, not replay the stale pre-wait marker as a fabricated 124 ‚Äî the round-4 regression), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-029 (boundary-tie race ‚Äî a leader that exits naturally at the deadline, before the watchdog has written any marker, must still defer to the watchdog via process-group liveness, not be cancelled outright ‚Äî the round-5 regression), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-032 (`mktemp` failure ‚Äî no marker file is ever possible, but the watchdog's own exit status still normalizes rc to 124/137 ‚Äî the round-6 regression), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-030/031 (source-location static assertion ‚Äî the abort lives in `lib-agent.sh` top-level code, never in `dispatcher-tick.sh`), TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-040/041 (remote-aws-ssm simulation ‚Äî local presence does not leak into the sourcing site's decision). E2E: `tests/e2e/run-error-envelope-e2e.sh` TC-TIMEOUTGUARD-060/061 (PATH-stubbed wrapper source + real `gh`-proxy envelope posting).

**Deferred (explicitly out of scope for #451)**:
- A cheap, best-effort, advisory-only `dispatcher-tick.sh` preflight (fast-fail before an SSM round-trip in `local`-equivalent deployments) is optional per the issue and NOT implemented in this PR. If added later, it MUST use a distinctly-named code (`ADT_CFG_TIMEOUT_TOOL_MISSING_LOCAL_PREFLIGHT`, never `ADT_CFG_TIMEOUT_TOOL_MISSING`) so the two sites are never conflated, and MUST be documented as non-authoritative ‚Äî a pass there never substitutes for this invariant's `lib-agent.sh`-side check.
- `lib-lane.sh`'s `_LANE_TIMEOUT_CMD`/`_lane_bounded` (used by `adt-gc.sh`'s `loginctl` probe) has the identical silent-degrade shape but a smaller blast radius (GC tooling, not the main agent-execution path) and is explicitly deferred to a follow-up issue.

**Cross-references**:
- [INV-13](#inv-13-wall-clock-cap-on-agent-invocations) ‚Äî the wall-clock cap this invariant's fail-closed default protects; INV-13 assumed a timeout binary was always resolvable, this invariant closes that gap.
- [INV-38](#inv-38-per-side-agent_launcher-precedence) ‚Äî the guard shape (source-time abort, `error_surface` + `error_peek_issue_arg` call convention) this invariant mirrors.
- [INV-72](#inv-72-config-class-failures-must-surface-on-the-issue-never-log-only) ‚Äî `ADT_CFG_TIMEOUT_TOOL_MISSING` is a config-class failure and surfaces accordingly (issue-comment when `--issue` is known, else dispatcher-alert).
- [INV-114](#inv-114-every-pipeline-initiated-kill-escalates-term--bounded-grace--sigkill-gated-on-groupscope-emptiness-never-on-leader-liveness-alone) ‚Äî the watchdog fallback's kill escalation follows the same TERM‚Üíbounded-grace‚ÜíKILL shape INV-114 defines.
- [INV-48](#inv-48-per-side-review-wall-clock-timeout-agentreviewtimeout-1h-default-with-browser-e2e-exclusion-and-timeout-veto) ‚Äî the review-side timeout-veto aggregation this invariant's rc normalization (124/137, never a raw signal-death status) must feed correctly; an un-normalized watchdog expiry would silently degrade a should-veto timeout into a dropped `unavailable` vote.

---

## INV-127: a review-side divergent-findings non-convergence (`REVIEW_CONVERGENCE_CAP` consecutive `failed-substantive` rounds where the severity ratchet's own P0/P1 floor is STILL failing) is detected and HALTED ‚Äî the breaker transitions `reviewing ‚Üí stalled` then posts ONE structured `reason=review-round-cap` report, gated on an already-`stalled` skip (does NOT gate on `may_stall_now`, mirroring [INV-122]'s own rationale)

_Triage (issue #236): [machine-checked: tests/unit/test-review-convergence-rules.sh]_

**Numbering note**: this invariant has now been renumbered TWICE due to
successive rebase collisions, per the repo's INV number collision convention
("first-merged keeps it; renumber-on-rebase-collision" ‚Äî see [INV-116]'s own
numbering note):
1. Drafted as INV-124 pre-rebase, before origin/main's #468 landed as INV-124
   for the unrelated PR-diff-size soft-cap signal. Renumbered to INV-126
   (INV-125 was already taken by #466's crashed-session recovery work).
2. Before THIS branch's own INV-126 could be rebased onto main, origin/main's
   #469 independently landed its OWN INV-126 (the wall-clock-timeout-binary
   fail-closed fix) first. Renumbered a second time to INV-127, the next free
   slot after main's INV-126.

**Rule**: alongside [INV-127]'s two siblings ‚Äî [INV-105] (dev-side zero-commit
inaction on a frozen head) and [INV-122] (a fixed-point repetition of the
E2E gate on an unchanged (head, rc) pair) ‚Äî this invariant closes the THIRD
non-convergence mode: the review agent(s) keep finding a genuine blocking
P0/P1 finding, round after round, even though the dev agent DOES push new
commits each round. The motivating case: a downstream consumer project's
"clean up E2E fixture accumulation" bug fix grew two production DELETE
routes during development, and the review loop ran **10 rounds over ~6
hours** without converging ‚Äî round themes escalated monotonically in
exoticness while each finding stayed individually valid, and the loop was
broken only by operator takeover. The severity-aware blocking ratchet
(issue #449's R1 requirement ‚Äî `shouldBlockFinding`, `lib-review-severity.sh`)
loosens the blocking floor as rounds progress (P0-P3 all block at rounds 1-2;
P0-P2 at 3-4; only P0/P1 at 5+), so if the review is STILL failing that
late-round floor, the PR is not converging toward mergeability no matter how
many more rounds run ‚Äî this invariant halts the loop rather than burning
rounds indefinitely.

**Where** (single insertion point): inside `autonomous-review.sh`'s existing
substantive-FAIL sub-path (`$AGGREGATE == "fail"`, the branch that also
computes `_AGG_DEV_ACTIONABLE` for [INV-92]), BEFORE that branch's existing
`emit_verdict_trailer "failed-substantive"` / `submit_request_changes` /
`itp_transition_state "reviewing" "pending-dev"` sequence
([`review-fail-substantive`](state-machine.md#transition-table)). A trip
short-circuits ALL of that via an early `exit 0` ‚Äî the existing `pending-dev`
routing never runs for a tripped round. Gated on `$AGGREGATE == "fail"`
specifically (not merely `$PASSED_VERDICT == false`) so the sibling
`all-unavailable` outcome (every agent exited clean but posted no verdict at
all ‚Äî no findings text, no severity to score) never reaches the trip logic.

**Distinct fingerprint from both siblings**:
- [INV-105] (dispatcher-side, `lib-dispatch.sh`) counts consecutive
  zero-commit dev-RESUME rounds against a FROZEN head ‚Äî a dev-side inaction
  signal. Moves `pending_dev ‚Üí stalled`.
- [INV-122] (review-wrapper-side, `lib-review-e2e.sh`) counts a repeated
  E2E-gate `fail` against an UNCHANGED `(head_sha, e2e_lane_rc)` pair ‚Äî a
  fixed-point-repetition signal, where the review agents never even ran.
  Moves `reviewing ‚Üí stalled`.
- INV-127 (review-wrapper-side, `lib-review-cap.sh`) counts consecutive
  `failed-substantive` review ROUNDS where the dev agent DID push new
  commits each round and the review DID run ‚Äî a divergent-findings
  non-convergence signal. Also moves `reviewing ‚Üí stalled`, declared as its
  own `review-round-cap-breaker` transition in `transitions.json` (same
  precedent [INV-122] itself established for reusing the `stalled` label
  without reusing an existing transition).

**Why the counter is head-AGNOSTIC (deliberately UNLIKE [INV-122]'s
fingerprint)**: [INV-122]'s counter resets whenever the head changes ‚Äî correct
for its trigger (a fixed-point repetition requires an UNCHANGED head). This
invariant's motivating case has a NEW head every round (the dev agent keeps
pushing fixes) ‚Äî a head-scoped counter would never reach any cap in that
exact scenario, defeating the breaker's purpose. So `_review_cap_next_count`
(`lib-review-cap.sh`) accumulates across CONSECUTIVE `failed-substantive`
rounds regardless of whether the head changed between them; the marker still
RECORDS the head at each round (forensic/audit value ‚Äî which head was under
review at each round), but does not use it as a reset key. This is also why
INV-127's round counter is a SEPARATE marker from R1's own
`review-round-counter` (`lib-review-round.sh`): that marker IS
head-scoped by design (it feeds the severity ratchet's blocking floor, which
correctly loosens for repeated re-review PRESSURE against unchanged code) ‚Äî
reusing it here would inherit the wrong reset semantics.

**State storage**: a structured HTML-comment marker posted by
`autonomous-review.sh`, following the `dispatcher-gate-fail-breaker` /
`dispatcher-convergence-breaker` marker convention ([INV-105], [INV-122]):

```
<!-- dispatcher-review-cap-breaker: issue=<N> head=<sha> round=<n> -->
```

Marker scan is **unbounded** ‚Äî full comment history via `itp_list_comments`
(mirrors [INV-105]/[INV-122] ‚Äî this breaker cannot tolerate a miss the way a
bounded-retry read can; losing the marker would silently re-enable the exact
loop this invariant exists to stop). The marker is computed and posted on
EVERY `failed-substantive` round (not only on a trip) ‚Äî embedded in the
ordinary "Review findings:" FAIL comment on a non-trip round ‚Äî mirroring
[INV-122]'s own "must persist every round" fix (the very first failure must
leave a marker for the next round to find, or the counter can never
advance). The marker read filters to `authorKind != "human"` (mirrors
[INV-105]/[INV-122]'s own marker-authenticity filter ‚Äî without it, any
collaborator able to comment on the issue could pre-seed a forged marker at
a high count and force the next genuine failure to trip prematurely).

**Cutoff on resume ([P1] codex review finding, fixed pre-merge)**: because
this counter is head-AGNOSTIC (see above), the marker scan is ALSO cut off at
the latest `reason=review-round-cap` trip report's `createdAt` ‚Äî mirroring
[INV-05]'s "Marking as stalled" cutoff convention
(`lib-dispatch.sh::count_retries` family). Without the cutoff, the marker
read would keep finding the OLD trip's own marker (embedded in that same
report) as the "latest" one forever, so the very next `failed-substantive`
round after an operator removes `stalled` to resume would compute
`round = threshold + 1` and re-trip immediately ‚Äî the resume flow could never
actually accumulate a fresh series. With the cutoff, any marker AT OR BEFORE
the last trip report is excluded from the scan, so a resumed round finds no
qualifying prior marker and starts a genuinely new count at 1. No trip yet ‚áí
the cutoff is the epoch and every marker counts, unchanged from the
pre-cutoff behavior.

**Reset on an intervening non-failing round ([P1] codex review round 3, #449,
fixed pre-merge)**: the trip-report cutoff above only excludes markers at or
before a past TRIP. It does not, by itself, reset the series when a
`Review PASSED` or `failed-non-substantive` round lands BETWEEN two
`failed-substantive` rounds ‚Äî no `dispatcher-review-cap-breaker` marker is
posted on a PASS or non-substantive round (the marker is posted only inside
the `$AGGREGATE == "fail"` substantive branch), so without an additional
cutoff the NEXT substantive-FAIL round would read the OLDER
pre-intervening-round marker back and resume counting from it, letting the
breaker trip on `REVIEW_CONVERGENCE_CAP` *total* substantive failures rather
than `REVIEW_CONVERGENCE_CAP` *consecutive* ones ‚Äî silently defeating the
"consecutive" half of this invariant's own fingerprint. Fixed by adding a
second cutoff input to `_review_cap_prior_marker`: the latest
authorKind!=human comment carrying a `<!-- review-verdict: ‚Ä¶ -->` trailer
whose verdict is `passed` or `failed-non-substantive` (anchored to those
literal tokens so it never matches `failed-substantive` itself). The
EFFECTIVE cutoff used for the marker scan is `max(trip_cutoff, reset_cutoff)`
‚Äî whichever boundary is more recent governs, so a reset that lands after the
last trip excludes an intervening marker even though the trip itself would
not have, and conversely a stale reset that predates the last trip is
correctly superseded by the trip cutoff. The `authorKind != "human"`
authenticity filter applies to the reset scan identically to the marker scan
‚Äî a collaborator quoting `<!-- review-verdict: passed -->` in an ordinary
comment cannot forge a reset.

**Reset-cutoff is FULL-BODY anchored, not a substring test ([CRITICAL,
silent-failure-hunter finding, fixed pre-merge])**: the reset-cutoff pattern
is `^<!--...-->[[:space:]]*$` ‚Äî it requires the trailer to be the ENTIRE
comment body, not merely present somewhere in it. A bare `test()` substring
match (the pattern's first-draft shape) would have let a genuine review
agent's OWN `Review findings:` FAIL body falsely satisfy the reset ‚Äî agents
are prompted to read all prior issue comments and can legitimately quote or
discuss an earlier trailer in prose (e.g. "the earlier `<!-- review-verdict:
passed -->` trailer turned out to be wrong"). Because that FAIL comment is
itself `authorKind != "human"` (bot/App-authored), the authenticity filter
above does not catch this case ‚Äî only the full-body anchor does. This is the
identical bug class `lib-dispatch.sh::authentic_verdict()` was hardened
against in its own round-13/14 fix history (a `startswith`-only match let
extra trailing content past the gate); `_review_cap_prior_marker`'s
reset-cutoff query reuses that anchored shape rather than reintroducing the
weaker substring form. `emit_verdict_trailer` always posts the genuine
trailer as a bare, standalone comment with no other text, so the anchor
never rejects an authentic reset while reliably excluding a trailer-shaped
substring embedded in a larger comment.

**Threshold**: `REVIEW_CONVERGENCE_CAP` (new env var, default `5`), read via
the same regex-then-fallback shape as [INV-122]'s `GATE_FAIL_STALL_THRESHOLD`
read (`lib-review-cap.sh::_review_cap_threshold`), plus an explicit floor of
`>=2` and a logged `WARNING:` on any fallback (non-numeric or `<2` ‚Üí falls
back to 5) ‚Äî the same explicit-floor-plus-warning posture [INV-122] adopted,
stricter than [INV-105]'s own silent threshold fallback.

**Only `failed-substantive` rounds count** ‚Äî `failed-non-substantive` is out
of scope (already governed by `REVIEW_RETRY_LIMIT` /
`count_review_aware_flips`, a structurally distinct counter this invariant
does not touch). The all-unavailable sub-path (no verdict at all) also never
reaches the trip logic (see the `$AGGREGATE == "fail"` gate above).

**An all-timeout `fail` also never reaches the trip logic ([P1] codex review
round 4, fixed pre-merge)**: `AGGREGATE == "fail"` is produced by
`_aggregate_review_verdicts` for TWO distinct causes ‚Äî a genuine per-agent
`fail` (a real, severity-scored blocking finding survived the ratchet) OR an
[INV-48] `timed-out` veto with no findings text at all (a reviewer hung and
was killed by its wall-clock cap). Both correctly block the merge, but only
the first is evidence the severity ratchet's own P0/P1 floor is "still
failing" ‚Äî a round where EVERY deciding fail is a bare timeout veto has no
agent-scored finding to point to. `_aggregate_has_substantive_fail`
(`lib-review-aggregate.sh`) makes this narrower distinction: "true" iff at
least one per-agent verdict is the literal token `fail` (post severity
filter), "false" if every deciding fail was `timed-out`. Both this
invariant's trip block and R1's `review-round-counter` marker post are gated
on `$AGGREGATE == "fail" && $_AGGREGATE_SUBSTANTIVE_FAIL == "true"` ‚Äî without
this, a run of transient per-agent timeouts on successive heads could
advance the SAME head-agnostic counter this breaker uses and eventually
stall a PR that no review agent ever actually found a live P0/P1 in.

**A P2/P3-only substantive fail ALSO never reaches the trip logic ([P1] codex
review round 7, fixed pre-merge)**: `_AGGREGATE_SUBSTANTIVE_FAIL == true`
confirms a REAL (non-timeout) fail survived the severity filter at the
CURRENT round's floor ‚Äî it does not say WHICH severity survived. This
invariant's own fingerprint is narrower: the ratchet's TERMINAL floor
(P0/P1) must still be failing, not merely "some fail survived at whatever
this round's floor happens to be." A P2 finding blocks (survives as `fail`)
at rounds 1-4, and R1's `review-round-counter` is head-SCOPED ‚Äî it resets to
1 on every new HEAD. This invariant's own counter is deliberately
head-AGNOSTIC because its motivating scenario has a NEW head every round
(see the "head-AGNOSTIC" design note above) ‚Äî so a PR that surfaces only P2
findings across a run of new heads would keep re-entering the round 1-4
floor (never reaching round 5+, since each new head resets R1's own
counter), yet its P2 `fail` would still satisfy the old
`_AGGREGATE_SUBSTANTIVE_FAIL` gate every round and eventually trip THIS
breaker despite no P0/P1 ever existing ‚Äî a false stall.
`_aggregate_has_p0p1_fail` (`lib-review-aggregate.sh`) closes this: given
each agent's (verdict, highest-severity) pair, it echoes "true" iff at least one `fail`
verdict's severity is NOT `P2`/`P3` (i.e. is `P0`, `P1`, `none`, or any
unrecognized token ‚Äî the same severities that still block under
`shouldBlockFinding`'s own round>=5 case arms). This invariant's trip block
now additionally requires `$_AGGREGATE_HAS_P0P1_FAIL == "true"`; R1's
`review-round-counter` marker post is UNCHANGED (it feeds "how many rounds
have run", not "is the terminal floor failing", so the broader
`_AGGREGATE_SUBSTANTIVE_FAIL` remains correct there).

**This gate's correctness depends on receiving a genuine severity, which the
codex path did not until [INV-132] (issue #481)**: `_aggregate_has_p0p1_fail`
above is fed the per-agent `AGENT_HIGHEST_SEVERITY[i]` values the severity
filter computed ‚Äî and pre-#481, that computation ALWAYS scored a codex
agent's RAW stdout capture (which always echoes the prompt's own untagged
checklist ahead of the agent's findings), collapsing to `none` regardless of
the agent's actual tags. `none` counts as P0/P1-class here BY DESIGN (an
unscoreable finding must never be excluded from the terminal floor) ‚Äî so a
codex-sourced P2-only round was misclassified as `none` and satisfied this
gate every round, tripping the breaker on a false "P0/P1 floor is still
failing" report even though every actual finding was `[P2]`. [INV-132] fixes
the UPSTREAM input (which text gets scored), not this gate's logic ‚Äî the gate
was always correct given a genuine severity; it simply never received one on
the codex path.

**Trip behavior**, gated in this order:

1. Check current issue labels for `stalled` FIRST ‚Äî if already stalled (e.g.
   [INV-105] or [INV-122] tripped first), do NOT re-trip or post a competing
   report. **[P1 fix, review round 5]**: the already-stalled check
   short-circuits the ENTIRE ordinary failed-substantive routing, not merely
   the trip report ‚Äî `exit 0` immediately after the check (no round-cap
   counter read/persist, no `emit_verdict_trailer`, no
   `submit_request_changes`, and critically no
   `itp_transition_state "reviewing" "pending-dev"`). The pre-fix code only
   skipped the trip-report branch and then fell through to the normal FAIL
   routing, which included the unconditional `pending-dev` transition at the
   end of the substantive-FAIL sub-path ‚Äî clobbering a sibling breaker's
   `stalled` label with a COMPETING `pending-dev` flip on the very same
   wrapper invocation, silently re-arming the loop the sibling breaker just
   halted (the issue would bounce `stalled ‚Üí pending-dev` despite no operator
   ever removing `stalled`). Exiting immediately leaves the sibling's
   `stalled` label as the sole, uncontested terminal state.
2. **No `may_stall_now` call** ‚Äî same rationale [INV-122] documents: this
   breaker runs synchronously INSIDE the very review wrapper the dispatcher
   just launched, so `may_stall_now` would always see this process's own
   fresh `review`-mode dispatch marker and defer for its full TTL, silently
   defeating the breaker for any FAIL completing within that window (the
   common case). The `reviewing`-label single-writer invariant already
   provides the liveness guarantee `may_stall_now` exists to add.
3. `itp_transition_state "$ISSUE_NUMBER" "reviewing" "stalled"` runs FIRST,
   atomically, BEFORE `RESULT_PARSED` is set ‚Äî mirrors [INV-105]/[INV-122]'s
   TOCTOU fix: a failed transition aborts the whole wrapper under
   `set -euo pipefail` before `RESULT_PARSED` is touched, so the
   crash-cleanup EXIT trap correctly treats it as a genuine crash rather than
   masking a landed stall.
4. `RESULT_PARSED=true` is set IMMEDIATELY after the transition lands, BEFORE
   the report post ‚Äî a transient failure in the report post must not leave
   `RESULT_PARSED=false`, which would make the crash-cleanup EXIT trap re-add
   `pending-dev` on top of an already-landed stall.
5. Post exactly ONE structured report (marker + human-readable body,
   `reason=review-round-cap`) summarizing the still-open P0/P1 findings, then
   `exit 0` ‚Äî never reaching the normal `failed-substantive`/`pending-dev`
   routing.

**Concurrency**: no new locking ‚Äî the existing `reviewing`-label
single-writer invariant (the flock-guarded PID-file guard) already rules out
two concurrent writers to the same issue's marker.

**Bias to MISS**: an absent, malformed, or non-matching marker collapses to
count=0 (never a crash, never a silent inherit of a garbled count). Because
of the resume cutoff above, an operator removing `stalled` re-arms a
genuinely FRESH series (round 1) regardless of how the underlying findings
were addressed ‚Äî the very next `failed-substantive` round does NOT re-trip
immediately; it takes another full `threshold` count of consecutive
`failed-substantive` rounds. This is a deliberate divergence from
[INV-105]/[INV-122]'s literal "removal re-arms the pipeline" mechanics (their
head-scoped/frozen-head fingerprints naturally reset on the next real change
without needing a report-timestamp cutoff); INV-127's counter is
head-AGNOSTIC by design (see above), so without the cutoff "removal re-arms
the pipeline" would be neutered ‚Äî the very first post-resume failure would
immediately re-read the old trip's own marker and re-trip.

**Status**: **ENFORCED**. New `transitions.json` entry
(`review-round-cap-breaker`, `reviewing ‚Üí stalled`) + regenerated
`state-machine.md` mermaid + prose row + `spec-guard-map.json` /
`spec-codesite-map.json` entries (`check-spec-drift.sh` Checks A/B/C all
green). Test plan: `docs/test-cases/review-convergence-rules.md`.

**Producer**: `autonomous-review.sh` (the substantive-FAIL sub-path) + pure
helpers in `lib-review-cap.sh` (`_review_cap_marker`, `_review_cap_parse_count`,
`_review_cap_next_count`, `_review_cap_threshold`, `_review_cap_prior_marker`).
The resume cutoff-then-scan (see above) is itself `_review_cap_prior_marker`
‚Äî a pure function over the full `itp_list_comments` JSON, not inlined at the
wrapper call site ‚Äî so its self-referential-exclusion behavior (the trip
report's own embedded marker must NOT satisfy the cutoff) is fixture-testable
rather than only wiring-greppable. Does NOT modify [INV-105]'s or
[INV-122]'s own fingerprint/trigger; does NOT source `lib-dispatch.sh` or
reuse `may_stall_now`/`pid_alive`/`get_pid` (same rationale [INV-122]
already established).

**Tests**: `tests/unit/test-review-convergence-rules.sh` (threshold/counter
pure-logic assertions + source-of-truth wiring greps against
`autonomous-review.sh`, mirroring [INV-122]'s
`test-e2e-gate-circuit-breaker.sh` two-pronged style).

**See also**:
- [INV-40] ‚Äî the unanimous-PASS aggregation this breaker's `$AGGREGATE ==
  "fail"` gate reads; unchanged.
- [INV-92] ‚Äî the per-finding dev-actionability classification computed in the
  same substantive-FAIL sub-path this breaker's trip check sits inside;
  unrelated axis, unaffected.
- [INV-105] ‚Äî the sibling breaker whose marker/threshold/report shape this
  invariant mirrors, for a DIFFERENT trigger (dev-side zero-commit inaction
  on a frozen head vs. review-side divergent findings across changing heads).
- [INV-122] ‚Äî the sibling breaker whose marker/threshold/report shape this
  invariant ALSO mirrors, for a DIFFERENT trigger (fixed-point E2E-gate
  repetition on an unchanged head vs. divergent findings across changing
  heads) ‚Äî and whose "must persist the marker every round" / "no
  `may_stall_now`" / "already-stalled skip" fixes this invariant inherits
  directly.
- #449 ‚Äî the issue that introduced the severity-aware blocking ratchet (R1)
  whose own P0/P1 floor this breaker's trigger condition depends on, the
  `review-round-counter` marker (also R1) this breaker's counter is
  deliberately NOT the same as, and the R3 evidence-freshness fix (narrower,
  unrelated to this breaker).

---

## INV-128: any non-terminal issue whose observable state fingerprint (label, PR head, non-idempotent comment count, marker digest) stays unchanged for `LIVENESS_NOTICE_TICKS` (default 6) consecutive dispatcher ticks gets ONE operator-visible tier-1 escalation, and after `LIVENESS_STALL_TICKS` (default 18) further unchanged ticks is unconditionally transitioned to `stalled` with a structured `reason=liveness-timeout` report ‚Äî the pipeline's first *global liveness* invariant: every non-terminal issue either changes observable state or is escalated within a bounded number of ticks

_Triage (issue #236): [machine-checked: tests/unit/test-liveness-watchdog.sh, tests/e2e/run-liveness-watchdog-e2e.sh]_

**Rule** (drafted as INV-127 pre-rebase ‚Äî see the numbering note below): five "permanent silent park" bugs shipped point-fixes in six months ([INV-105], [INV-111], [INV-122], [INV-123], [INV-125]): the issue's label is legal and stable (usually `pending-dev`), but the dispatch decision layer falls into an absorbing loop that posts one idempotent notice and then no-ops every tick ‚Äî no retry, no `stalled`, no operator mention. Each fix so far is a per-entry patch keyed on a specific park shape (a specific stop reason, a specific classifier branch, a specific marker fall-through). A multi-model design review (8 reviewers, 4 with live repo access) unanimously concluded the entry set is **open** ‚Äî reboots clearing `/tmp` logs, new provider stop reasons, new CLI adapters, and future marker fall-throughs all mint new entries, so enumeration can never finish. This invariant closes the *class*: it does not know or care WHY an issue stopped making progress, only THAT it did, for how long, and what the observable evidence looked like at each tick.

**Where** (single insertion point): a new tick step, `run_liveness_watchdog`, called from `dispatcher-tick.sh` as **Step 6**, immediately after Step 5's stale-detection loop and before the end-of-tick metrics prune. Pure fingerprint/counter/threshold/tier-decision helpers live in `lib-liveness.sh` (mirrors `lib-review-diffcap.sh`/`lib-review-classify.sh`'s pure-decision-surface pattern); the orchestration (`_liveness_evaluate_issue`/`run_liveness_watchdog`) ‚Äî the ONLY code that performs the watchdog's `label_swap` ‚Äî lives in `lib-dispatch.sh`, deliberately NOT in `lib-liveness.sh`: `check-spec-drift.sh`'s Check C only scans four hardcoded files (`autonomous-dev.sh`, `autonomous-review.sh`, `dispatcher-tick.sh`, `lib-dispatch.sh`) for label-write sites, so a write site in a fifth, unscanned lib would be invisible to the spec-drift gate ‚Äî passing trivially without actually validating the new movement. Running after Step 5 means `JUST_DISPATCHED` (already exported as a scalar by that point) also protects any issue Steps 2-4 dispatched THIS tick from being double-counted as a no-op.

**Scope (this iteration)**: `pending-dev` and `pending-review` only ‚Äî the existing selectors `list_pending_dev`/`list_pending_review` (which already exclude `approved`/`stalled`) supply the candidate set, so the terminal-label exemption is structural, not a separate runtime check. `in-progress`/`reviewing` are already covered by Step 5b's DEAD-process scans (`dispatcher-flow.md` ¬ß Step 5b) ‚Äî extending the watchdog there risks double-escalation with Step 5b's own crash-declaration comments and is deferred. This scoping directly closes the documented gap that Step 5b never scans `pending-dev`/`pending-review` ‚Äî the exact blind spot every observed park has exploited.

**Numbering note**: this invariant has been renumbered due to a rebase
collision, per the repo's INV number collision convention ("first-merged
keeps it; renumber-on-rebase-collision" ‚Äî see [INV-116]'s own numbering
note): drafted and originally merged-locally as INV-127, before origin/main's
concurrently-merged #470 (severity-ratchet review convergence) independently
claimed INV-127 for its own, unrelated invariant first. Renumbered to
INV-128, the next free slot after main's INV-127.

**PRIMARY trip signal ‚Äî the state fingerprint.** `_liveness_fingerprint(label, pr_head_sha_or_empty, non_idempotent_comment_count, marker_digest)` hashes the pipe-delimited canonical string (mirrors [INV-105]'s `convergence_trailer_hash` shape: `sha1sum | cut -c1-12`, `cksum` fallback). Each component:

- `label`: the active pipeline label passed in by the caller (`pending-dev` or `pending-review`) ‚Äî not re-derived per tick, since the candidate list itself already reflects it.
- `pr_head_sha_or_empty`: via the existing `fetch_pr_for_issue` verb; empty when no PR. **[codex review, PR #472, round 8 BLOCKING #3]** `fetch_pr_for_issue` is fail-CLOSED on transport error (`resolve_pr_for_issue`/`chp_find_pr_for_issue` propagate a nonzero rc on a `gh` failure ‚Äî only rc=0 with an empty result means "genuinely no PR bound"). `_liveness_evaluate_issue` checks that rc BEFORE reading the head: a transport failure defers the ENTIRE tick (same posture as the `itp_list_comments` preflight below), never treating "the lookup failed" as "there is no PR" ‚Äî the pre-fix code collapsed both into `current_head=""`, which on an issue that DOES have a PR would silently reset the counter on a transient API blip, masking a park exactly on the tick meant to detect it.
- `non_idempotent_comment_count`: total comments MINUS comments matching `_LIVENESS_IDEMPOTENT_PATTERN` ‚Äî a single grep-able list covering every known idempotent-notice/marker grammar this repo's breakers/self-heal paths post (`stale-verdict:`, `INV-12-completed:`, `INV-12-no-pr-fresh-dev:`, `INV-35-fresh-dev:`, `no-progress-substantive(-attempt)?:`, `non-actionable-finding:`, `self-heal-lost-session:`, `self-heal-non-substantive:`, `crashed-session-retry:`, `crashed-session-non-actionable:`, `dispatcher-convergence-breaker:`, `dispatcher-gate-fail-breaker:`, `dispatcher-token:`, `INV-25-hygiene:`, the watchdog's own `dispatcher-liveness-watchdog:`, and ‚Äî [round 6] since the tier-1/tier-2 human-readable reports are now separate comments from the bare marker (see below) ‚Äî `reason=liveness-no-progress`/`reason=liveness-timeout` themselves) ‚Äî a park's first notice must NOT reset the clock, or the park would never be detected (the exact failure mode this invariant exists to close). **[codex review, PR #472, round 8 BLOCKING #2]** Every grammar is matched with a leading wrapper anchor (requiring a backtick code span or an HTML-comment opening immediately before the token) ‚Äî the pre-round-8 bare alternation was an unanchored substring test that a human comment merely discussing or quoting a token in prose (e.g. "I saw reason=liveness-timeout mentioned somewhere") satisfied identically to a genuine wrapped marker/report, wrongly EXCLUDING that prose comment from the count. **[codex review, PR #472, round 10 BLOCKING]** The round-8 anchor only required the wrapper's OPENING delimiter ‚Äî a backtick or `<!--` immediately before the token ‚Äî with no check that the span ever CLOSED, so a human comment that
